topics.topic: Gen Z students are arriving at college unable to read a full sentence, professors warn

self_relevance.rationale: Touches every major that assigns reading, and ties directly into how students study and use AI to shortcut texts.

comparison_potential.rationale: Splits by major (humanities vs STEM), by generation (Gen Z vs Millennial reading habits), and by how professors at different schools are adapting.

decision_usefulness.rationale: More of an awareness/self-check than an active decision, though it can prompt students to rethink study habits.

share_trigger.rationale: Sharp, identity-poking stat that students send to friends either to mock or defend themselves.

comment_trigger.rationale: Highly polarizing - 'this is so true' vs 'this is insulting and overblown' debate is almost guaranteed.

format_strength.rationale: Shock-stat hook card plus myth-vs-reality framing about AI summaries replacing reading.

freshness.rationale: Originated from a January 2026 Fortune piece that is still actively being syndicated and discussed across outlets.

evidence_availability.rationale: Multiple independent outlets (Fortune, Essence, Futurism) plus a cited YouGov reading-habits statistic.

topics.score_summary: Today's most novel and highest-scoring topic: a genuinely surprising, widely-corroborated claim with strong share/comment potential and no overlap with recently covered themes.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #1: highest combined buzz and freshness of the day, a shock-value stat that beats #2 (housing) on comment-trigger and novelty, while still resting on solid multi-outlet evidence.

sources.title: Gen Zers are arriving to college unable to even read a sentence — professors warn it could lead to a generation of anxious and lonely graduates
sources.summary: Professors report students unable to read full sentences; reading habits down ~40% over a decade; students lean on AI summaries instead of doing assigned reading.
sources.relevance: Primary, named-source reporting with direct professor quotes and YouGov reading-habit data.

sources.title: College Professors Warn Of A Literacy Crisis As More Gen Z Students Struggle With Reading
sources.summary: Independent coverage corroborating the literacy-crisis framing and professor adaptations (reading aloud in class).
sources.relevance: Independent outlet corroborating the same professor-reported pattern.

sources.title: Gen Z Arriving at College Unable to Read
sources.summary: Further independent syndication/analysis of the same literacy-crisis reporting.
sources.relevance: Third independent outlet covering the same claim, adding to source diversity.

buzz.reason: Picked up and actively discussed across multiple national outlets (Fortune, Essence, Futurism, Yahoo, Fox News) within months.

velocity.reason: Originated January 2026 and still being actively re-shared/discussed as of late June.

target_fit.reason: Relevant to nearly every current and incoming student across majors.

content_potential.reason: Strong shock-stat hook plus myth-vs-reality angle about AI-summary shortcuts.

source_diversity.reason: Three independent outlets, though largely tracing back to the same original Fortune reporting and YouGov stat.

evidence_quality.reason: Named professor quotes and a cited YouGov statistic, but anecdotal/qualitative rather than a dedicated academic study.

risk_penalty.reason: No identifiable individual students named; professors quoted are discussing patterns, not singling out students.

topics.content_angle: Shock-stat hook: 'Professors say Gen Z can't read a full sentence' — paired with the AI-summary-shortcut angle and a myth-vs-reality format (what's actually happening vs the exaggerated headline).
topics.expected_reaction: Strong, polarized comments — some students relating/admitting it, others pushing back that it's an unfair generalization; high share potential to call out friends.
avoid: Don't present this as confirmed fact about 'all' Gen Z — frame as a professor-reported pattern backed by a real but limited dataset (YouGov reading stat), not a definitive study of literacy levels.
Avoid mocking tone; treat it as a structural/systemic discussion (testing culture, phone habits, AI shortcuts) rather than insulting the audience.

topics.topic: Off-campus housing has overtaken tuition as the bigger cost for many college students

self_relevance.rationale: Housing cost touches every student regardless of major, school, or year.

comparison_potential.rationale: Splits cleanly into dorm vs off-campus vs by-region dollar comparisons.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Directly informs where-to-live decisions for incoming and returning students.

share_trigger.rationale: Natural to send to roommates/parents currently negotiating a lease.

comment_trigger.rationale: Invites 'my rent is worse than that' comparison comments.

freshness.rationale: Persistent, currently-reported national cost shift, not a one-off old story.

evidence_availability.rationale: Two independent national education-news outlets with specific dollar figures.

topics.score_summary: Highest-evidence-quality topic today: broad self-relevance, strong comparison format, and solid independent dollar-figure evidence.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #2, just behind the literacy-crisis topic on novelty/comment-trigger, but ties or beats it on self-relevance, decision-usefulness, and evidence quality.

sources.title: Why many college students are forced to spend more on housing than tuition
sources.summary: Students at public four-year institutions pay an average of $12,770 in room and board vs $11,260 in tuition and fees.
sources.relevance: National-broadcast confirmation housing now outpaces tuition.

sources.title: The fastest-growing college cost? It's not tuition
sources.summary: Off-campus housing at 4-year public schools averages ~$10,781/year (~$1,080/month).
sources.relevance: Specific dollar figure for off-campus housing.

sources.title: North America's off-campus housing platform serving over 245 campuses and 3.5 million students
sources.summary: References the ongoing student housing shortage across North American campuses.
sources.relevance: Platform-native confirmation of housing-shortage chatter.

sources.title: Commentary on a $3,100 rent figure cited as a 'human rights violation'
sources.summary: Breaks down per-roommate rent split and attributes cost to supply shortage.
sources.relevance: Shows real-time public debate over student rent figures.

buzz.reason: National coverage from PBS plus a specialist education outlet, reinforced by social debate.

velocity.reason: Steady, ongoing national cost-shift story rather than a single viral spike.

target_fit.reason: Affects essentially every US college student making a housing decision.

source_diversity.reason: Two independent education-news outlets plus two social posts for color.

evidence_quality.reason: Specific, named dollar figures from two independent national outlets.

risk_penalty.reason: No identifiable individuals, no sensitive content.

topics.content_angle: 'Housing now costs more than your tuition' — real dollar comparison card by living situation (dorm vs off-campus vs by region).
topics.expected_reaction: Students comparing their own rent/dorm cost against the national averages and tagging roommates.
avoid: Don't frame as financial advice; present as a cost-comparison fact card, not budgeting guidance.

topics.topic: Your school might be #1 in the country for something hilarious: Princeton Review's 2026 'weird' college superlatives

self_relevance.rationale: Every student already has an opinion about their own school's party/food/vibe reputation, so this maps directly onto identity.

comparison_potential.rationale: The entire format is school-vs-school by category — exactly the comparison structure the audience shares and argues over.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Helps prospective students weigh fit (social scene, food, substance climate) beyond academic rankings alone.

share_trigger.rationale: Built-in 'tag your school' mechanic — students send it to current/future classmates the moment their school appears.

comment_trigger.rationale: Guaranteed 'mine should be #1' and 'this is so accurate/wrong' comment threads for every category.

format_strength.rationale: Swipe-card Top 3-by-category format is one of the strongest, most replicable formats in the playbook.

freshness.rationale: Drawn from the current (34th) edition of Princeton Review's 2026 guide, based on survey data from the last three academic years.

evidence_availability.rationale: Backed by an official survey of 170,000+ students at 391 schools, cross-checked against Niche's independently compiled 2026 category rankings.

topics.score_summary: A genuinely novel angle on 'college rankings' that sidesteps the generic 'Top 10 Best Universities' trap by using specific, surprising, official categories built for comparison and sharing.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #3, just ahead of the commuter-majority topic: comparable self-relevance but a stronger built-in share/comment mechanic ('tag your school') and a cleaner, more replicable format, offset slightly by being based on a guide released last fall rather than this week's news.

sources.title: Best Colleges 2026 | College Ranking List
sources.summary: Official 2026 guide ranking the top 25 schools in 50 student-survey-based categories, from Best Career Services to Lots of Hard Liquor and Reefer Madness.
sources.relevance: Primary official source for all category rankings and methodology.
sources.reliability: Long-running, named higher-ed rankings publisher; methodology based on surveys of 170,000+ students at 391 schools.

sources.title: Princeton Review's 2026 Best Colleges: Top Schools For Professors, Career Services — And Even 'Hard Liquor'
sources.summary: Compiles top-3 schools across both parent-facing and student-facing categories, with named schools and specific data points (e.g. Tulane #1 for Lots of Hard Liquor, University of Vermont for Lots of Beer/Reefer Madness, U.S. Merchant Marine Academy for Campus Food Not So Tasty).
sources.relevance: Independent secondary write-up with full category tables, useful for picking the most surprising/shareable categories.

sources.title: 2026 College Rankings
sources.summary: Independently compiled 2026 category rankings (Top Party Schools, Best College Food, Best College Campuses, etc.) corroborating that category-based, survey-driven college rankings are an active, ongoing content genre, not just a Princeton Review one-off.
sources.relevance: Cross-source corroboration that this ranking format/genre is current and active across multiple ranking providers.

buzz.reason: The 'weird' categories (hard liquor, reefer madness, food rankings) reliably resurface in social/media coverage each cycle; Niche's parallel rankings show sustained interest in the format.

velocity.reason: Guide was released last fall, so it's not breaking news, but the categories remain evergreen and shareable year-round.

target_fit.reason: Directly about choosing/validating a school — core to the audience's identity and decision-making.

content_potential.reason: Extremely strong, easily repeatable swipe-card format across dozens of categories.

source_diversity.reason: Two independent publishers (Princeton Review, Poets&Quants) plus a third independent ranking provider (Niche) corroborating the genre.

evidence_quality.reason: Based on an official, large-scale, named-methodology student survey (170,000+ respondents, 391 schools).

risk_penalty.reason: School-level survey data, not about identifiable individuals; no sensitive content.

topics.content_angle: Swipe-card series through the funniest/most surprising 2026 category rankings (Lots of Hard Liquor — Tulane, Reefer Madness — Vermont, Campus Food Not So Tasty — Lawrence Tech, Most Beautiful Campus — University of the South) with a 'tag your school' CTA on each slide.
topics.expected_reaction: Students tagging their own school the moment it appears in any category, plus pushback comments from schools that feel a category is unfair or outdated.
avoid: Don't present any single category as an overall judgment of school quality — frame each as one specific, survey-based data point.
Avoid a mocking/roast tone toward any named school; keep it playful and comparison-driven, not insulting.

topics.topic: Most college students are commuters now, not the stereotypical dorm-living undergrad

self_relevance.rationale: The majority of US college students are commuters, making this directly relevant to most of the audience.

comparison_potential.rationale: Varies sharply by school type (UC vs CSU vs community college).

decision_usefulness.rationale: Less of an active decision than an identity-validating reality check.

share_trigger.rationale: Commuter students sharing to validate an often-overlooked experience.

comment_trigger.rationale: Strong 'wait, that's actually me' / 'no way, not at my school' debate potential.

format_strength.rationale: Stat-led reality-check card format works well.

freshness.rationale: Underlying stats are evergreen rather than tied to a single new event.

evidence_availability.rationale: Two education-news sources, though exact percentage varies slightly (85% vs 86%) across them.

topics.score_summary: Strong, broadly relevant reality-check topic with a high comment-trigger but a less urgent decision angle than the top topics.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #4 on the strength of near-universal self-relevance and comment-bait potential, behind the three higher-novelty/evidence topics above.

sources.title: 10 Universities Where Most Freshmen Commute
sources.summary: Ranks universities by share of freshmen who commute rather than live on campus.
sources.relevance: Named, school-by-school comparison data.

sources.title: More students are going to college. Affordability and workforce training are factors
sources.summary: Community college enrollment rose ~3% in fall 2025, with affordability driving commuter enrollment.
sources.relevance: Ties rising commuter/community-college enrollment to affordability.

sources.title: You save money but pay with your mental health #college #university #commuterlife
sources.summary: Viral creator video on the financial-vs-wellbeing tradeoff of commuter life.
sources.relevance: Direct student-voice framing of the tradeoff.

buzz.reason: Steady education-press coverage plus an actively circulating TikTok framing of the tradeoff.

velocity.reason: Evergreen stat, no sharp recent spike.

target_fit.reason: Directly describes the actual majority-experience of US college students.

source_diversity.reason: Two education-news outlets plus one creator video.

evidence_quality.reason: Solid but slightly inconsistent percentage figures across sources (85% vs 86%).

risk_penalty.reason: No identifiable individuals, no sensitive content.

topics.content_angle: 'You're not the exception' — commuter-majority reality-check card paired with the financial-vs-wellbeing tradeoff.
topics.expected_reaction: Commuter students validating their own experience; dorm-living students surprised by the actual majority share.
avoid: Avoid stating one single precise percentage as definitive since sources differ slightly (85% vs 86%) — present as 'the large majority.'

topics.topic: Roommate conflict goes mainstream: campus papers and Netflix's 'Roommates' both spotlight dorm tension

self_relevance.rationale: Roommate friction is a near-universal dorm-living experience.

comparison_potential.rationale: Some school-to-school variation in RA support, but mostly a shared experience.

comment_trigger.rationale: Invites 'this happened to me' and 'my roommate did worse' comments.

format_strength.rationale: Myth-vs-reality format with the Netflix release as a timely hook.

freshness.rationale: Netflix film tie-in gives it a current pop-culture hook even though the underlying theme is familiar.

evidence_availability.rationale: Two independent campus-newspaper pieces corroborate the same structural pattern.

topics.score_summary: High share/comment potential; this theme previously appeared once before, but is past its cooldown window and has a fresh pop-culture hook.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #5 on strong share/comment metrics; placed behind the higher-novelty/self-relevance topics above, and because the theme repeats one last covered 2026-06-19.

sources.source_name: The Spectrum (University at Buffalo student newspaper)
sources.title: Living together, falling apart: the reality of roommate conflict
sources.summary: RA notes roommate conflict peaks early in the year before boundaries are set.
sources.relevance: First-person student newspaper account.

sources.title: COLUMN: Netflix's 'Roommates' exacerbates the experience of roommate collision
sources.summary: Connects the April 2026 Netflix film 'Roommates' to real dorm tension and roommate-matching anxiety.
sources.relevance: Pop-culture tie-in plus real student anxiety.

buzz.reason: Two campus-paper pieces plus a current Netflix release driving conversation, but not a major news event.

velocity.reason: Slow-building seasonal/perennial topic, refreshed by the Netflix hook.

target_fit.reason: Extremely relevant to incoming freshmen and dorm-living undergrads.

content_potential.reason: Strong myth-vs-reality and share-bait format potential.

source_diversity.reason: Two independent campus newspapers, but no national outlet or social data.

evidence_quality.reason: Corroborating but campus-newspaper-level evidence, not large-scale survey data.

risk_penalty.reason: Framed structurally; no identifiable individual's personal conflict is named.

topics.content_angle: Myth vs. reality on roommate conflict, tied to the Netflix release as a timely hook.
topics.expected_reaction: Students tagging roommates, sharing their own conflict stories in comments.
avoid: Do not reference any individual student's specific private conflict; keep all examples structural/anonymized.
Note for the report: this theme previously appeared in the 2026-06-19 brief ('Common roommate red flags students report').

topics.topic: Transfer students routinely lose academic credits, costing time, money, and sometimes their degree path

self_relevance.rationale: Highly relevant for transfer students specifically, less so for the general undergrad audience.

comparison_potential.rationale: Credit-loss rate varies sharply by transfer type (community college vs for-profit vs public).

decision_usefulness.rationale: Directly informs what to verify before transferring.

share_trigger.rationale: Worth sending to anyone actively planning a transfer.

comment_trigger.rationale: Some 'this happened to me too' potential, less debate-driving than other topics.

format_strength.rationale: Checklist format fits well ('what to verify before you transfer').

freshness.rationale: Underlying survey data is not brand-new, though the Texas case example is from March 2026.

evidence_availability.rationale: National survey plus a concrete state-level case study.

topics.score_summary: Solid, decision-useful topic for a specific but sizeable audience segment (transfer students).
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #6: strong decision-usefulness and evidence, but narrower target audience than the topics above.

sources.title: Survey: 3 in 10 Students Lose Significant Academic Credits Transferring Between Colleges
sources.summary: Roughly 3 in 10 transfer students lose significant academic credits in the transfer process.
sources.relevance: Named survey data quantifying a common pain point.

sources.title: Thousands of Texas Students Lose College Credits, Costing Time and Money
sources.summary: Over 19,000 Texas community college students didn't get credit for at least one course transferring in 2023-24; 16% gave up on higher ed.
sources.relevance: Concrete state-level numbers and a serious downstream consequence.
sources.reliability: Regional outlet citing state education data.

sources.title: Transfer and Learning Mobility in 2026 and Beyond
sources.summary: Opinion column on persistent structural barriers to credit transfer entering 2026.
sources.relevance: Frames the problem as ongoing and structural.

buzz.reason: Covered by a regional outlet and a professional association survey, not viral but consistently reported.

velocity.reason: Steady structural issue, not a fast-moving news spike.

target_fit.reason: High relevance for the transfer-student segment specifically.

source_diversity.reason: Three independent education-news sources covering different angles (survey, state case, opinion).

evidence_quality.reason: Named survey plus concrete state-level figures.

topics.content_angle: 'What to check before you transfer' checklist built around the credit-loss statistics.
topics.expected_reaction: Transfer students sharing the post with friends mid-transfer process; some recounting their own credit-loss experience.
avoid: Don't generalize the Texas-specific 19,000 figure as a national number — keep it labeled as a Texas case study.

topics.topic: Internship market gets more competitive while unpaid internships pay off far less

self_relevance.rationale: Relevant to most undergrads weighing internship options.

comparison_potential.rationale: Paid vs unpaid comparison, less variation by school/major in the data available.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Directly informs whether to pursue paid vs unpaid internships.

share_trigger.rationale: Useful to share with peers job-hunting, moderate urgency.

comment_trigger.rationale: Some 'I got ghosted too' potential but not highly debate-driving.

format_strength.rationale: Paid-vs-unpaid outcome comparison card works well.

freshness.rationale: Primary supply/demand stat is from Feb 2025 — over a year old, the most current available but not new.

evidence_availability.rationale: One named research-forum-based source plus one secondary aggregator.

topics.score_summary: Decision-useful but relies on a stat that is now over a year old; flagged for verification before treating as breaking.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #7: solid decision-usefulness offset by dated underlying data and below-28 rubric total, so flagged needs_verification.

sources.title: 12% of college students won't participate in an internship
sources.summary: 8.2 million students wanted to intern but 4.6 million couldn't get a spot; paid interns get offers at 66.4% vs 43.7% for unpaid.
sources.relevance: Direct demand/supply gap and outcome gap.
sources.reliability: Trade publication citing named research forum.

sources.title: Internship Statistics U.S. 2026
sources.summary: ~60.8% of US internships paid vs 39.2% unpaid; Handshake postings fell 15% from 2023 to 2025.
sources.relevance: Supply/demand divergence framing.

buzz.reason: Covered by a trade publication citing a named research forum, moderate reach.

velocity.reason: Underlying stat is over a year old; low current velocity.

target_fit.reason: Relevant to most undergrads considering internships.

source_diversity.reason: One primary trade-publication source and one weaker secondary aggregator.

evidence_quality.reason: Solid named data but dated (Feb 2025), no fresher figure available.

risk_penalty.reason: No identifiable individuals, no sensitive content.

topics.content_angle: Paid-vs-unpaid outcome comparison card with the supply/demand squeeze as the hook.
topics.expected_reaction: Students job-hunting comparing their own application-to-offer experience against the cited gap.
avoid: Label the core supply/demand stat as 2025 data, not a brand-new 2026 finding.

topics.topic: Greek life rush registration hits record highs even as younger students stay skeptical of frat/sorority culture

self_relevance.rationale: Only directly relevant to students considering Greek life.

comparison_potential.rationale: Data is largely single-school (UVA); limited cross-school comparison available today.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Somewhat useful for students weighing whether to rush.

share_trigger.rationale: Highly shareable within the Greek-life/RushTok community specifically.

comment_trigger.rationale: Invites 'my school's rush is nothing like that' comparisons.

format_strength.rationale: 'Rush by the numbers' card format works well.

freshness.rationale: Underlying registration data is from February 2026, not brand-new.

evidence_availability.rationale: Single-school primary source supplemented only by aggregated TikTok volume, not a second named data point.

topics.score_summary: Engaging niche topic, but generalizing from a single school's registration numbers and aging data limits today's confidence.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #8 — lowest of today's ranked set due to single-school evidence base and a narrower target audience than the other topics.

sources.source_name: The Cavalier Daily (University of Virginia student newspaper)
sources.title: Fraternity and sorority recruitment sees influx in registration
sources.summary: 1,100 women and 825 men registered for spring 2026 rush at UVA — IFC's largest sign-up since extending its deadline; ISC's largest-ever sorority membership (3,082).
sources.relevance: Concrete, named-school registration numbers.

sources.title: RushTok — TikTok discover page
sources.summary: Active 'RushTok' discover topic aggregating recruitment content from sororities across many campuses.
sources.relevance: Confirms sustained platform-native volume.
sources.reliability: Aggregated discover page; trend-volume color only.

sources.title: i show you my introduction video I submitted in the pre rush prep club! #sororityrecruitment #rushvideo #rushtok
sources.summary: Creator-native rush-prep advice video circulating under the #rushtok tag.
sources.relevance: Direct creator evidence of active student engagement.

buzz.reason: Single-school news plus active RushTok hashtag volume, but no national data point.

velocity.reason: Spring rush season content is ongoing but the core data point is from February.

target_fit.reason: Niche relevance — mainly students considering or already in Greek life.

content_potential.reason: Good numbers-card potential, but limited by single-school framing.

source_diversity.reason: One primary campus-news source plus aggregated/single-creator TikTok content only.

evidence_quality.reason: Single-school numbers generalized cautiously; no second named institution for comparison.

risk_penalty.reason: No identifiable individuals named, no sensitive content.

topics.content_angle: 'Rush by the numbers' card pairing the UVA registration surge with what RushTok shows about the process.
topics.expected_reaction: Greek-life-affiliated students sharing and comparing against their own school's numbers.
avoid: Clearly label the registration figures as UVA-specific, not a national trend, until a second school's data is found.

excluded_topics: c01_student_loan_overhaul (in cooldown until 2026-06-27, covered 4x in past week)
c03_major_regret_job_market (in cooldown until 2026-06-26, covered 5x in past week)
c06_ai_cheating_arms_race (in cooldown until 2026-06-28, covered 3x in past week)
